definition: A structural pattern that decouples an abstraction from its implementation so the two can vary independently. It splits a class into two orthogonal class hierarchies: an abstraction that holds a reference to an implementor interface, allowing independent extension of both sides.
ruleOfThumb: When you need to support orthogonal dimensions of variability (e.g., shapes × rendering backends), prefer an abstraction that delegates to an implementation interface rather than creating a subclass for every combination.

citation.author: Erich Gamma, Richard Helm, Ralph Johnson, John Vlissides
citation.bookTitle: Design Patterns: Elements of Reusable Object-Oriented Software

questions.prompt: Which of the following best captures the primary intent of this structural approach?

options.text: Separate an abstraction from its concrete implementations so both hierarchies can be extended independently.
options.explanation: Correct — the pattern introduces an implementor interface that the abstraction delegates to. This avoids a combinatorial explosion of subclasses by allowing each axis to evolve on its own.

options.text: Convert one incompatible interface into another so legacy code can be reused by new clients.
options.explanation: Incorrect — that describes an adapter-style translation that wraps an interface to match another, not the separation of abstraction and implementation into parallel hierarchies.

options.text: Attach additional responsibilities to an object dynamically by wrapping it with decorator objects.
options.explanation: Incorrect — decorators add behavior at runtime by chaining wrappers, while this structural approach separates roles so both can be extended independently.

options.text: Provide a surrogate or placeholder for another object to control access to it.
options.explanation: Incorrect — that matches the proxy concept (controlling access), which is different from splitting abstraction and implementation into orthogonal hierarchies.

questions.prompt: Given the code below, which statement most accurately describes the relationship between Shape and Renderer?

options.text: The shape delegates drawing to a separate implementor interface so new shapes and new renderers can be added independently.
options.explanation: Correct — Circle holds a Renderer reference and delegates rendering calls. This lets you add more Renderer implementations or more Shape subclasses without creating one class per combination.

options.text: This is purely a strategy for selecting a runtime algorithm; shape and renderer are the same axis of variation.
options.explanation: Incorrect — although Strategy and this approach both use composition, here the intent is to maintain two orthogonal hierarchies (shape types vs rendering backends), not merely to swap algorithms for a single behavior axis.

options.text: Renderer is an adapter that translates Circle calls to platform-specific drawing APIs.
options.explanation: Incorrect — while a renderer may wrap platform specifics, an adapter's intent is interface translation for compatibility. The code shows a deliberate separation to allow independent extension, not only translation.

options.text: Circle is a factory that produces renderer instances based on radius.
options.explanation: Incorrect — Circle receives a Renderer via constructor injection and delegates to it; it doesn't instantiate or choose renderers based on state, so it isn't acting as a factory.

questions.prompt: Which version better supports adding new shapes and new rendering backends independently?

options.text: Version A is better because it composes a renderer with shapes, enabling independent extension of both hierarchies.
options.explanation: Version A keeps shape responsibilities separate from rendering implementations by composition: a single Renderer interface can be implemented in many ways while Shape subclasses focus on geometry. This avoids combinatorial subclasses (one class per shape × backend), reduces duplication, and isolates changes to one axis.

options.text: Version B is better because one class per shape-backend pair allows each combination to optimize its drawing code without indirection.
options.explanation: While direct classes can optimize per combination, Version B scales poorly: adding a new renderer requires adding N subclasses for N shapes, increasing maintenance and coupling. It also scatters rendering concerns across many classes rather than centralizing backend-specific code.
